text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

A US anti-doping chief called the 23 Chinese swimmers who tested positive 'a deep betrayal of athletes who compete fairly'

The integrity of the World Anti-Doping Agency (Wada) has been called into question over a doping scandal which continues to rage aroundChinese swimming.

In late 2020 and early 2021, 23 elite Chinese swimmers tested positive for trimetazidine, known as TMZ, a heart medication classified among the most serious category of performance-enhancing drugs.

But they continued to compete, including at theTokyo Olympicsin 2021, after the China Anti-Doping Agency (Chinada) determined all 23 cases were the result of contaminated food at Huayang Holiday Hotel in Shijiazhuang, where the swimmers were staying.

Athletes who had tested positive went on to claim three gold medals inTokyo, including Wang Shun, who became just the second Chinese man to win an individual swimming gold in the 200m individual medley.

Qin Haiyang, expected to beAdam Peaty’sclosest rival in the 100m breaststroke, also tested positive in 2021, alongside 12 other Chinese swimmers competing inParis.

Here’s everything you need to know about the row which could overshadow these Olympic Games.

While at a New Year’s event in Shijiazhuang, a city of 11 million people not far from Beijing, 60 tests were conducted on 39 swimmers.

These 60 tests produced 28 positive samples from 23 of the competitors, all for TMZ.

The 61-page Chinada report said investigators discovered traces of TMZ in spice containers, cooktop vents and sink drains in the team hotel, although it has never been established how that was transmitted to the swimmers.

Wada general counsel Ross Wenzel has said Chinada “didn’t hypothesise” why trace amounts of a prescription heart medication were found all over the kitchen of the team hotel.

All positive samples should be sent toWadaandWorld Aquatics(then known as Fina) immediately, but these tests were frozen for two and a half months before being delivered to Wada, reportedly due to the Covid-19 pandemic.

The anti-doping body chose not to act further having “carefully reviewed the decision” and spoken to external lawyers and scientists.

The 23 swimmers then competed in Tokyo and the positive results only became public knowledge in April this year, after an investigation byThe New York Timesand German news outletARD.

As Wada came to the conclusion there was no wrongdoing, president Witold Banka said: “It’s a question about whether you want or not to expose the innocent athletes, right?”

The Timesreported that World Aquatics only shared the evidence with Dr Jordi Segura, chairman of its doping control Review Board – none of the rest of the committee were informed of the positive tests.

This was despite international anti-doping officials providing Wada with intelligence appearing to show a cover-up of doping by Chinese swimmers, including an email exchange between a Chinese official and a senior world swimming official suggesting a violation may have taken place.

ARDalso revealed recently that there is evidence not all athletes were staying in the same hotel, while some of those who tested positive did not find out until the investigation was published in April.

A further independent investigation concluded in July that Wada had not shown bias in their handling of the case, despite their senior director of science and medicine Olivier Rabin having “doubts about the reality of contamination as described by the Chinese authorities.”

Yet Mr Rabin said he “saw no solution than to accept it” as Wada was “not in a position to disprove” Chinada’s version of events.

One of the bodies who has given Wada allegations of Chinese doping on multiple occasions in the past four years is the US anti-doping body Usada.

Usada CEO Travis Tygart toldTheNew York Times: “This appears to be a devastating stab in the back of clean athletes and a deep betrayal of all the athletes who compete fairly and follow the rules.

“All of those with dirty hands in burying these positives and suppressing the voices of courageous whistleblowers must be held accountable to the fullest extent of the rules and law.”

Usada and the FBI are currently conducting an investigation of their own into Chinese doping under the authority of a US law, the 2019 Rodchenkov Anti-Doping Act, which allows nations to conduct cross-border investigations.

Yet the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and Wada have suggested they could stop the United States from hosting future Olympics if the FBI do not end their investigation.

Wada plans to review the Rodchenkov Act after the Games, which it says has created an “unfair” parallel anti-doping system.

This is an extraordinary act of strong-arming from the governing bodies, especially when the US have been awarded the 2028 Summer Games in Los Angeles and the 2034 Winter games in Salt Lake City.

This has led Mr Tygart to call Wada the IOC’s “lapdog”, while Mr Banka said the relationship with Usada is “difficult”.

Some of the loudest voices in this row have been current and former swimmers from across the globe.

Michael Phelpssaid: “As athletes, our faith can no longer be blindly placed in the World Anti-Doping Agency, an organisation that continues to prove that it is either incapable or unwilling to enforce its policies consistently around the world.

“It is clear to me that any attempts of reform at Wada have fallen short, and there are still deeply rooted, systemic problems that prove detrimental to the integrity of international sports and athletes’ right to fair competition, time and time again.”

On Wednesday, seven-time US gold medallist Katie Ledecky said: “Athletes want transparency. They want further answers to the questions that remain. We want to see some change for the future so that you don’t have to ask us that question.”

There was similar sentiment from Australian Zac Stubblety-Cook, whose 200m breaststroke world record was beaten by Qin last year.  “It feels like [Wada has] failed and that’s the truth,” he said.

“I absolutely believe in clean sport and I hope that this is a clean Games. It’s obviously disappointing to hear about the positive tests of 23 athletes; in some cases multiple positives. And, for me, finding out that you’re racing someone who was one of those athletes is disappointing.”

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: The doping scandal engulfing the Paris 2024 Olympics
author: George Simms
description: A US anti-doping chief called the 23 Chinese swimmers who tested positive 'a deep betrayal of athletes who compete fairly'
